turns.content: summarize this article. I am interested in the argument, challenges to conventional understanding, and contributions to the scholarship on iamblichus and his connection with hermeticism and hieratic art.

Here’s a structured summary of Kyle Fraser’s article “Hermetism and Hieratic Tradition in the De mysteriis of Iamblichus” (GRBS 65, 2025), focusing on the argument, challenges to conventional understanding, and contributions to scholarship: Argument Fraser argues that Iamblichus’ De mysteriis should be read not just as a defense of theurgy against Porphyry’s criticisms but as a deeply Hermetic-inflected vision of hieratic tradition . By assuming the persona of the Egyptian prophet Abamon, Iamblichus aligns himself with the Hermetic convention of pseudonymous priestly voices and subordinates his own authority to that of Hermes Trismegistus. Central to Iamblichus’ defense of theurgy is the claim that divine union cannot be achieved by intellect alone (the Plotinian–Porphyrian model) but only through ritual mediation : sacred rites, hieratic traditions, invocations, and the ineffable “barbarous names.” These names are not meaningless; they participate in the divine order itself. Fraser shows that Iamblichus explicitly links these practices to the Hermetic “way of Hermes,” where ritual speech and sacred language enable communion with the gods. The paper further argues that Iamblichus’ anxiety over translation and linguistic corruption —his insistence that invocations lose their power when rendered into Greek—finds a precise parallel in Corpus Hermeticum XVI. Both sources deploy the Greek term ἐξίτηλος (“fading,” “ineffectual”) to describe how translation dilutes the power of ritual speech, suggesting Iamblichus was familiar with, or at least operating within the same priestly discourse as, this Hermetic tractate. Challenges to Conventional Understanding 1. Against the standard Platonic reading : Porphyry and the Plotinian tradition cast theurgy as irrational, a departure from philosophy’s contemplative purity. Iamblichus challenges this by claiming that reliance on intellect alone actually blocks salvation—since union with the divine surpasses knowledge and can only occur through ritual communion ( συναφή ). This undermines the “contemplation-only” model of Platonism. 2. Reassessment of Hermetism’s role : Hermetism is usually seen as parallel to, but separate from, Neoplatonism. Fraser shows instead that Hermetic ritual theories directly shaped Iamblichus’ theology of ritual language. The overlap is not incidental but integral: Iamblichus considered Hermetic texts to preserve authentic Egyptian priestly traditions, even in Greek translation. 3. Translation as dilution : Whereas scholarship often treats the Hermetic lament about language (CH XVI) as rhetorical, Fraser situates it as part of a broader priestly worldview, shared with Iamblichus, that equates linguistic assimilation with ritual weakening. This reframes the Hermetica less as philosophical allegory and more as guardians of endangered ritual praxis. Contributions to Scholarship Lexical Discovery : Fraser highlights the overlooked parallel use of the adjective ἐξίτηλος in both De mysteriis and Corpus Hermeticum XVI. This provides strong evidence of Hermetic influence on Iamblichus’ theology of language. Hermetic Contextualization of Theurgy : The article shows that Iamblichus’ defense of “barbarous names” and hieratic rites belongs within the same Egyptianizing discourse as the Hermetica, bridging Neoplatonism and Hermeticism rather than keeping them apart. Reframing Iamblichus’ Persona : By analyzing the pseudonym “Abamon” as a Hermetic-style literary device, Fraser suggests that Iamblichus was consciously presenting himself not as an innovator but as a transmitter of an authentic, ancient, priestly tradition. Contribution to debates on magic vs. theurgy : Fraser demonstrates that Porphyry’s dismissal of ritual as “magic” misrepresents Iamblichus’ position, which depends on an alternative metaphysics of divine immanence. Theurgy is not coercive but participatory. Cultural/Linguistic Insight : The essay situates Iamblichus and the Hermetica in the liminal position of Hellenized Egyptian priesthoods , grappling with the decline of hieroglyphic literacy and the encroachment of Greek, while still striving to preserve hieratic ritual efficacy. ✅ In sum : Fraser makes a strong case that Iamblichus’ De mysteriis should be read as part of the same Hermetic–priestly milieu that produced the Corpus Hermeticum . His emphasis on ritual language, hieratic transmission, and pseudonymous authority demonstrates a shared cultural project: resisting the dilution ( ἐξίτηλος ) of sacred tradition under Hellenism by insisting on the irreducible efficacy of Egyptian ritual speech and practice. Would you like me to also map Fraser’s contribution against earlier key scholars (like Fowden, Shaw, Bull, and Frankfurter) so you can see exactly where his interpretation advances or challenges the field?
walk me through the reassessment of hermeticism's role and map it against contributions of earlier scholars

Great question. Here’s a tight walkthrough of Fraser’s reassessment of Hermeticism’s role in De mysteriis and how it lines up (or argues past) major scholars. 1) Hermeticism is not parallel but constitutive of Iamblichus’ theurgy Fraser shows Iamblichus presenting himself as a transmitter of the “way of Hermes” : he writes as the prophet Abamon , subordinates his own authority to Hermes Trismegistus , and treats Hermetic books as carriers of authentic Egyptian priestly wisdom that underwrites theurgic soteriology. This recasts De mysteriis as embedded in a Hermetic–hieratic milieu rather than merely defending ritual to a Platonist audience. fileciteturn0file4 fileciteturn0file16 2) Hermetic books as Greek translations of the Books of Thoth (and thus liturgical manuals) Iamblichus explicitly frames the Hermetica as Greek translations of Thoth’s writings that preserve ritual protocols —i.e., they are the textual conduit of hieratic practice, not philosophical ornament. That bridges Iamblichus’ theurgy to concrete Hermetic liturgy. fileciteturn0file6 fileciteturn0file4 3) Language ideology: ritual speech is energetic, translation is de-potentiating Fraser’s key move is the lexical link between De myst. and CH XVI : both use ἐξίτηλος (“made ineffectual/fading”) to condemn how translation into Greek drains the power of Egyptian invocations. Iamblichus’ defense of onomata barbarā lines up directly with Hermetic claims that Egyptian names carry the energy of the beings named , whereas Greek eloquence is mere ψόφος (“babble”). This repositions Hermetism as a theory of efficacious language foundational to theurgy. fileciteturn0file10 fileciteturn0file18 4) Shared ritual technologies Fraser maps Iamblichus’ immaterial offerings and name-chanting to Hermetic speech-offerings , vocalic chants , and visionary ascent (e.g., Discourse on the Eighth and Ninth ), stitching a common ritual repertoire rather than two unrelated traditions. fileciteturn0file17 5) Cultural–theological stakes He contrasts Porphyry’s Hellenic universalism (and “magic” polemic) with Iamblichus’ local, priestly traditionalism , then pairs Iamblichus’ insistence on divine presence (παρουσία) with the Hermetic Asclepius apocalypse predicting cultic abandonment when rites are suppressed. This frames Hermeticism as the ideological ally of Iamblichean hieratic resistance. fileciteturn0file14 fileciteturn0file13 Garth Fowden – _The Egyptian Hermes_ What Fowden established: the historical embeddedness of the Hermetica in Egyptian priestly culture and their liturgical color; he coined the category of “theological Hermetica.” fileciteturn0file11 Where Fraser pushes further: he uses Iamblichus’ own framing to argue Hermetic texts functioned (for Iamblichus) as authoritative ritual-linguistic sources for theurgy, not just a comparable literature. He tightens the fit via the ἐξίτηλος bridge to CH XVI, something not thematized in earlier overviews. fileciteturn0file10 On priestly authorship: Fowden was noncommittal on whether Hermetic authors were priests; Fraser notes that later work (Bull) leans priestly, and he aligns Iamblichus with that priestly horizon. fileciteturn0file2 Gregory Shaw – _Theurgy and the Soul_ What Shaw emphasized: the Neoplatonic logic of theurgy— visualization, synthēmata, name-chanting , and non-discursive union—with occasional geometric visualization. fileciteturn0file1 Fraser’s addition: he grounds those practices in Hermetic comparanda (speech-offerings, vocalics, the Eighth and Ninth hymn), arguing Iamblichus’ theurgy is Hermetically theorized about language and ritual efficacy, not only Neoplatonically rationalized. fileciteturn0file17 Christian H. Bull – _The Tradition of Hermes Trismegistus_ What Bull argued: strong case for an Egyptian mise-en-scène and probable Hellenized priestly authorship ; detailed structural/motif parallels (e.g., _Poimandres_). fileciteturn0file15 Fraser’s uptake: he builds on Bull’s priestly-historical picture to show that Iamblichus self-positions as a priestly transmitter (Abamon) and that Hermetic hierarchies and ascent help explain Iamblichus’ gradated sacrificial theory (immaterial vs. material). fileciteturn0file4 fileciteturn0file19 David Frankfurter – _Religion in Roman Egypt_ What Frankfurter contributed: the sociolinguistic shift (Greek/Old Coptic in priestly/liturgical settings) and the pressures on temple culture under Rome. fileciteturn0file5 Fraser’s use: he leverages Frankfurter’s data to historicize CH XVI and Iamblichus’ anxieties: translation as ritual dilution is not rhetoric alone but a priestly response to linguistic blending—thus explaining Iamblichus’ insistence on non-translated names . fileciteturn0file9 B. Copenhaver (ed./trans.) & related philology Baseline: stabilized the Hermetic corpus and highlighted Asclepius on statue consecration and immaterial worship. fileciteturn0file1 Fraser’s spin: uses these touchstones to correlate Iamblichus’ immaterial cult for higher gods with Hermetic worship protocols and discursive suspicion of merely syllogistic Greek. fileciteturn0file19 fileciteturn0file18 Adamson / O’Meara (lost Hermetica & Egyptian theology) What they add: pathways from lost Hermetica and Chaeremonic astral lore to Iamblichus’ Egyptian theology. fileciteturn0file11 fileciteturn0file15 Fraser’s synthesis: these lines support the claim that Iamblichus’ hierarchy and soteriology are informed by a Hermetic doctrinal archive , not just Greek Platonism. fileciteturn0file6 Bottom line Fraser reframes Hermeticism from a neighboring tradition to the matrix of Iamblichus’ theurgy—textually (Abamon’s deference to Hermes; Hermetica-as-translations-of-Thoth), ritually (shared vocalics/speech-offerings/immaterial sacrifice), linguistically (CH XVI’s ἐξίτηλος as the key to Iamblichus’ anti-translation stance), and ideologically (defense of divine presence against Porphyrian universalist rationalism). In doing so he consolidates and extends Fowden/Bull’s historical-priestly picture, grafts Shaw’s theurgic mechanics onto Hermetic theories of language , and anchors it all in Frankfurter’s account of Roman-Egyptian cultural pressures. fileciteturn0file4 fileciteturn0file10 fileciteturn0file17 fileciteturn0file9 If you want, I can turn this into a one-page lit-review grid (claims / sources / Fraser’s move) for quick reference.
give me a close reading of the material from Iamblichus that Fraser uses, including exact quotations with chapter/verse citations

Perfect — let’s get very textual. Fraser leans heavily on De mysteriis in Books 1, 5, 7, and 8. I’ll walk you through the passages he cites, give you the Greek terms , exact quotations with citations , and how he uses them in his argument. 1. On knowledge vs. contact with the divine ( De myst. 1.3 [5.21–6.2]) Iamblichus sets his anthropology/theology against Porphyry’s intellectualism. “Indeed, to speak truthfully, our contact (συναφή) with divinity is not even a form of knowledge (γνῶσις). For [knowledge] is, in a certain respect, separated [from its object] by otherness.” ( Myst. 1.3 [5.21–6.2]) Fraser stresses that gnosis is separation , but syna­phē is union . This is a key move: salvation is not epistemic but ritual/participatory, opening the door for Hermetic-style theurgy. 2. Divine immanence through hieratic mediation ( De myst. 1.3 [7.6–8]) On how the soul is joined to the gods: “…the soul can only be joined (συνηρτημένη) to the gods through pure and irreproachable intellections which it has received from all eternity from the gods.” ( Myst. 1.3 [7.6–8]) Fraser uses this to show that for Iamblichus, the divine initiative and hieratic mediation trump philosophical ascent. 3. The tradition of Hermes and Egyptian origins ( De myst. 7.5 [191.23–24]) Iamblichus roots theurgy in Egyptian cults: “…for they [the Egyptians] were the first to be allotted communion (μετουσίαν) with the gods.” ( Myst. 7.5 [191.23–24]) Fraser emphasizes that Iamblichus positions himself not as innovator but as transmitter of Hermetic–Egyptian hieratic tradition . 4. Invocation of Hermes and priestly pseudonymity ( De myst. 1.1 [2.17]; [3.1–24]) Opening of the treatise, Iamblichus speaking as Abamon: “…pious subordination to Hermes Trismegistus, the god who presides over rational discourse, and the common patron of all priests…” ( Myst. 1.1 [2.17–3.1]) and “…our ancestors in particular dedicated the fruits of their wisdom, attributing all their own writings to Hermes.” ( Myst. 1.1 [3.20–24]) Fraser reads this as deliberate Hermetic pseudonymity: like the Hermetica, De myst. erases authorial ego in favor of priestly transmission. 5. On immaterial vs. material sacrifice ( De myst. 5.19 [168.15–24]) A hierarchy of sacrifices: “Whenever we offer cult to the gods who rule over soul and nature, it is not inappropriate to offer them natural powers… But when we endeavour to honour those gods that are in and of themselves singular in form, it is fitting to bestow on them honours that are not bound to matter.” ( Myst. 5.19 [168.15–24]) Fraser pairs this with Hermetic immaterial worship ( Asclepius 41). 6. Defense of barbarous names ( De myst. 7.4–5 [189.9–192.22]) Porphyry’s challenge (quoted by Iamblichus): “Τί γὰρ βούλεται τὰ ἄσημα ὀνόματα;” – “What is the point of the meaningless names?” ( Myst. 7.4 [189.9–10]) Iamblichus’ reply: “…although these names are meaningless to humans, to the gods they are all significant (τοῖς μέντοι θεοῖς πάντα σημαντικά ἐστιν), ineffably united (ἀφθέγκτως… συνηνωμένος) to them.” ( Myst. 7.4 [189.13–19]) On foreign divine names: “But,” you say, “the listener looks to the meaning (τὰ σημαινόμενα), so that as long as the concept (ἔννοια) remains the same, it is sufficient, no matter what name is used.” But the situation is not as you suppose. For if the names were established by convention, it would not matter whether some were used instead of others. But if they are joined to the nature of what exists (τῇ φύσει συνήρτηται τῶν ὄντων), then those that are better adapted to it will be more precious to the gods.” ( Myst. 7.5 [190.26–191.7]) And on translation: “…even if one could translate them, they would not retain the same power (δύναμιν). For the barbarian names possess weightiness and great precision… with less ambiguity… This is the reason that all these things… have become ineffectual (ἐξίτηλα), both the names and the prayers, because they are endlessly altered according to the inventiveness of the Hellenes.” ( Myst. 7.5 [191.13–192.22]) This is the lexical hinge of Fraser’s article: ἐξίτηλα appears in both De myst. and CH XVI to describe ritual dilution through translation. 7. Theurgy vs. Porphyry’s rationalism ( De myst. 1.8 [21.10–16]) Iamblichus accuses Porphyry’s stance of desacralizing the cosmos: “…this doctrine constitutes the ruination of sacred ritual and theurgical communion of gods with men, by banishing the presence (παρουσίαν) of the higher beings outside the earth. It amounts to saying the divine is set apart… and that this realm is bereft (ἔρημος) of divinity.” ( Myst. 1.8 [21.10–16]) Fraser connects this with the Hermetic Asclepius 24–26, where Egypt is prophesied to be abandoned by the gods. 8. Hermetic cosmology in Egyptian terms ( De myst. 8.2–3 [195.14–23]) Iamblichus’ summaries of Hermetic theology: The indivisible One (τὸ ἓν ἀμερές) → self-begotten Monad (νοητάρχης; identified as Kmeph). From this Monad: Demiurgic Mind, veiled in mythological names of Amoun, Ptah, Osiris. Fraser shows that Iamblichus draws directly on lost “theological Hermetica.” 1.3 : replaces gnosis with synaphē → ritual over intellect. 1.1 : invokes Hermes, adopts pseudonym Abamon → Hermetic pseudonymity. 5.19 : sacrifices adapted to hierarchy → echoes Hermetic immaterial worship. 7.4–5 : defense of ineffable/barbarous names → ἐξίτηλος as anti-translation keyword. 1.8 : Porphyry’s rationalism = cosmic desacralization → paralleled in Asclepius . 7.5 & 8.2–3 : Hermetic theology embedded in De myst. cosmology. Fraser’s originality is in seeing all these not as incidental but as evidence that Hermetic hieratic theory of ritual language underwrites Iamblichus’ theology of theurgy. Do you want me to juxtapose these Iamblichean passages directly with the matching Hermetic excerpts (CH I, XIII, XVI; Asclepius ) the way Fraser does, so you can see the textual echo and contrast point-by-point?

Great—here’s a tighter, deeper map of Fraser vs. earlier scholarship (set 1 of 3): Fowden, Shaw, Bull. 1) Garth Fowden — The Egyptian Hermes (1986) Core theses (Fowden): The Hermetica reflect a Hellenized Egyptian priestly wisdom; “Egyptian” color is real but mediated through Greek language/philosophy; the cultic/ritual dimensions (speech-offerings, temple piety, statue-consecration) are integral to Hermetic soteriology. The Old Model (Preisendanz → Betz) The Preisendanz edition (1928–31; 2nd ed. 1973–74) and the English PGM in Translation (ed. Hans Dieter Betz, 1986; rev. 1992) presented the PGM as a cohesive anthology : - “The Greek Magical Papyri” were treated as a literary corpus of late antique spells, roughly uniform in genre. - Implicit narrative: these are the remnants of Egyptian temple magic , preserved in papyri caches. - Betz framed them as a window into “magic” in antiquity, complementing Hermetica, Orphica, etc. 🔑 Problem: This flattened disparate material into a single “corpus,” ignoring textual instability, codicology, and the diverse origins of the papyri. 2. The Newer Consensus (2000s onward) Scholars like David Frankfurter, Jacco Dieleman, and others pushed against this monolithic view. Key shifts: - Not a unified corpus: “PGM” is a modern scholarly construct , gathering manuscripts from multiple provenances, dates (2nd c. BCE to 5th c. CE), and languages (Greek, Demotic, Coptic). - From ‘spells’ to ‘handbooks’: Many PGM items are ritual formularies or handbooks — working notebooks compiled by scribes, priests, or ritual specialists, not free-floating texts. - Codicological turn: Instead of isolating individual spells, the focus is now on entire codices , their organization, marginalia, and use-contexts. Scholars treat PGM papyri as artifacts of scribal and ritual practice , not just containers of “texts.” - Hybridity as essential: The mixture of Greek invocations, Egyptian voces, Jewish and Christian names, etc., is seen as a defining feature , not “corruption.” 3. Current Cutting-Edge Directions The “Magical Formularies” Project (Daniel Kölligan, Joachim Quack, et al.) is reconstructing whole magical codices rather than excerpted “PGM texts.” - Goal: to show how scribes structured collections of rituals and how handbooks circulated. Expanded boundaries: What counts as “PGM” is broadening — integrating Demotic Magical Papyri (PDM), Coptic handbooks, and even late amulets and ostraca into the same study framework. The old strict “PGM vs. PDM vs. Coptic magical texts” is breaking down into a single ritual-literacy continuum . Authority strategies: Scholars (e.g. Edmonds, Frankfurter) emphasize how these texts construct legitimacy by invoking antiquity, secrecy, voces magicae, and names of gods. This overlaps with Fraser’s Iamblichus/Hermetism connections. PGM as “lived religion”: Instead of a marginal “magical” underworld, the PGM are increasingly read as part of normal religious practice — embedded in healing, divination, protection, and temple ritual technologies. 4. Implications for Fraser & Iamblichus Fraser’s stress on language efficacy, untranslatability, and immaterial cult looks even stronger when set against the PGM-as-handbooks model. - The same anxieties about names ( ἄρρητα ὀνόματα , voces magicae) and language loss ( ἐξίτηλος ) run through Hermetica, Iamblichus, and the papyri. Was there a "PGM spirituality" similar to what Wouter Hanegraff calls Hermetic Spirituality?

Great question — and yes, scholars have asked almost exactly this. Here’s how it looks if we map Wouter Hanegraaff’s “Hermetic spirituality” (an inner, experiential, soteriological religion of ascent, transformation, and gnosis, recovered especially from CH XIII and related texts) against what we can reasonably reconstruct as a “PGM spirituality.” 1. Hanegraaff’s “Hermetic spirituality” Core: inward purification, rebirth of the nous, ascent to God, and the experience of becoming divine ( theosis ). Means: contemplative prayer, hymnody, visionary gnosis. Goal: ontological transformation and union ( synousia ) with the divine. Features: interiorized cult (hymns instead of sacrifices), immaterial worship, metaphysical ascent through the spheres. 2. What the PGM give us Recent scholarship (Frankfurter, Betz, Dieleman, Smith, Johnston, Faraone) stresses that the PGM are not just “technical magic” — they also construct a worldview and a ritual subjectivity . We can call this a “PGM spirituality” if we recognize its contours: a) Initiatory and visionary rites PGM texts include “Mithras liturgy” ( PGM IV.475–829), PGM I.262–347, PGM XIII.1–343, etc. These are not short spells but full-scale ascent rituals : visionary journeys through the cosmic layers, culminating in seeing the divine light, becoming immortal, or being “reborn.” Scholars (e.g., Betz, Meyer, Smith, Johnston) have long pointed out that these overlap heavily with Hermetic visionary gnosis. b) Rebirth and transformation The Mithras liturgy instructs the practitioner to cry: “Born again, I, having been begotten by you in the house of the spirit!” ( γεννάομαι… ἀναγεγεννημένος ). Compare Hermetic CH XIII: γεννηθέντι σε, τέκνον, ἐν τῷ νοῒ — “you are born, my child, in mind.” The “spiritual rebirth” motif is shared between Hermetica and PGM. c) Union with God / the Stars PGM rites often end with union or identification with cosmic powers: “You are one, you are lord, you are I and I am you.” This resembles Hermetic monism: CH XI: to know God is to become God. d) Piety, not just coercion While many PGM spells are coercive (love charms, curses), the longer hymnic sequences cultivate piety: “Holy are you, O God… Holy are you…” (PGM II, PGM XIII). These look structurally identical to the Hermetic hymns Fraser highlights ( ἅγιος εἶ ). The same logic of hymnic ascent and immaterial offering is at play. 3. Scholarly framing Older scholarship saw the “Mithras liturgy” and related PGM visionary spells as foreign anomalies in a body of otherwise “technical magic.” Current view: they are part of the same ritual-literacy continuum — demonstrating that the PGM include not just “practical” but also mystagogical, transformative, and initiatory texts. Scholars now talk about “ritual spirituality” (Smith, Johnston, Frankfurter) where ritual handbooks are tools for transformation, not just manipulation. 4. Conclusion: Was there a “PGM spirituality”? Yes — but embodied differently from Hermetic spirituality. - Hermetism encodes a discursive, philosophical spirituality : dialogues, theology, inner ascent. - The PGM encode a ritualized spirituality : handbooks of techniques (invocations, names, hymns, rites) that enable ascent, rebirth, union, and divine vision. Both traditions share key motifs: rebirth ( παλιγγενεσία ), hymnic praise ( ἅγιος εἶ ), ineffable names ( ἄρρητα ὀνόματα ), divine union ( συνουσία ), and immaterial worship. Where Hermetism spiritualizes cult into philosophy, the PGM ritualize spirituality into handbooks . ✅ Answer in a sentence: Yes, the PGM do exhibit a “spirituality” parallel to Hanegraaff’s “Hermetic spirituality” — not contemplative discourse but ritual spirituality , in which hymns, names, and visionary rites guide the practitioner to rebirth, divine union, and immortality. Modern Egyptology has made it increasingly clear that what we call “PGM spirituality” or “Hermetic spirituality” didn’t just spring up in the Roman era, but grows out of long-standing Egyptian ritual–theological traditions. Let me sketch the key continuities: 1. Ritual Language and the Power of Names Concept: mdw-nṯr (“divine words”) in Egyptian thought are not descriptive but performative — uttering the right word effects reality. Evidence: Coffin Texts, Pyramid Texts, and temple inscriptions emphasize secret names of gods, which grant the speaker power over them. Continuity: This logic reappears in the PGM voces magicae , the Hermetic ἄρρητα ὀνόματα , and Iamblichus’ insistence that Egyptian names cannot be translated without losing efficacy ( ἐξίτηλος ). Scholarly framing: Jan Assmann and Antonio Loprieno stress that this is not “magic” as separate from religion — it is a fundamental cosmological semiotics in Egyptian theology. 2. Theurgy and Divine Union in Egyptian Terms Concept: Ritual is not symbolic but an operative link (often called “sympathetic” but better: participatory ) between human and divine. Earlier evidence: - Coffin Texts and Book of the Dead describe the deceased becoming Osiris, Ra, or a star. - Divine statues and cult images were not representations but living embodiments of deities, activated by rites like the “Opening of the Mouth.” Continuity: The PGM and Hermetica both describe divine visions, transformations, and rebirths . The “Mithras liturgy” (PGM IV) is essentially a ritualized ascent that echoes Pyramid Text ascensions to the sky. Fraser’s angle: Iamblichus systematizes this in Neoplatonic theurgy — but the underlying ritual ontology is very old in Egypt. 3. Immaterial Offerings and Pure Worship Older Egyptian practice: While animal sacrifice existed, elite Egyptian theology always stressed the primacy of incense, hymns, and prayer — “offerings of Maat” (truth/order) — over bloody ritual. Continuity: - The Instruction for Merikare and temple texts: “Better is bread with Maat than oxen with wrongdoing.” - Daily temple ritual included hymnic recitations as key offerings. In Hermetism / PGM: Asclepius 41 insists the highest offering is immaterial hymns and prayers , echoed in Iamblichus’ Myst. 5.19. So: This Hermetic–Iamblichean “spiritualized sacrifice” is not Greek invention , but a late antique continuation of Egyptian high theology. 4. Initiation and Scribal Pedagogy Temple “House of Life” (pr-ʿnḫ): scribal schools where initiates learned texts, chants, rituals. The Book of Thoth in Demotic shows dialogues between master and disciple about divine names and sacred writing. Continuity: Hermetic dialogues mirror this master–student instruction model. Iamblichus, too, presents himself as revealing “the Egyptian wisdom of Abammon” in De mysteriis . So: Hermetism and Iamblichus are both plugged into a pedagogical tradition of hieratic discourse . 5. Cosmogony and Theological Speculation Earlier Egyptian cosmogonies: - The Heliopolitan Ennead (Atum → Shu/Tefnut → Geb/Nut …). - Memphite Theology ( Shabaka Stone ): Ptah as creator through heart and tongue . - Hermopolitan Ogdoad (Nun, Amun, etc.). Continuity: Hermetic cosmogonies ( CH I, CH IX) and Iamblichus’ Egyptian theology ( Myst. 8.2–3) recast these: Monad, Demiurgic Mind, Osiris, Ptah, Amoun. So: Fraser’s claim that Hermetism provides Iamblichus with an Egyptian theological vocabulary aligns with Egyptological consensus that these cosmogonic speculations were still circulating in priestly milieus of Roman Egypt. 6. Magic as Normalized Temple Technology Older Egyptology: “Magic” was marginal, unofficial, or popular. New consensus: Ritual handbooks, healing spells, execration rites, and protective formulas were core temple technologies . They appear in tombs, coffins, amulets, and temple libraries. Continuity: The PGM are not anomalies but fragments of this larger tradition, adapted into multilingual Greco-Egyptian ritual handbooks. So: Fraser is right to treat Hermetism + Iamblichus + PGM as different expressions of the same hieratic continuum . 🗝️ Takeaway There was indeed a “PGM spirituality” , but its roots go much deeper: Already in Pharaonic theology, ritual language was performative, hymns were immaterial offerings, cosmogonies framed the gods as accessible through names, and ritual union with deities was a standard goal (at least for the king and the dead). The Hermetica and the PGM are late antique crystallizations of this same priestly worldview, recoded in Greek and hybridized with Platonic categories.
